Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place this semester. I'm 29, working part-time, and juggling a lot of responsibilities, so I couldn't stick to a perfect schedule. I tried to carve out about 25 hours a week, usually in chunks whenever I could find the time—early mornings before work, late nights after everything else was done. I'd print out readings and make handwritten notes because that's how I learn best, even though it meant I wasn't always logged into the system.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening in my life outside of school. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying every morning. Other weeks, especially when stress hit hard, I'd barely touch anything until a deadline forced me to. I tried to be regular, but life doesn't always cooperate when you're a non-traditional student with bills to pay.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was checking if there were new assignments posted or if deadlines were coming up. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials so I could study them offline later. I also checked discussion boards occasionally to see if anyone had questions I could relate to, though I didn't post as much as I probably should have.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more routine, especially at the start of the semester when I was more optimistic. I'd check in every few days out of habit. But honestly, that routine fell apart when my stress levels spiked and I started feeling overwhelmed. The CBT sessions helped me recognize that pattern, but building consistent habits while managing everything else was really hard.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because of my learning style—I retain information better when I read and write things down. I'd download PDFs and articles, then annotate them by hand. The videos were helpful too, though I often watched them at 1.5 speed and took notes separately rather than rewatching them multiple times.
Q6: I often ignored the discussion boards and some of the optional supplementary videos. It wasn't that they weren't valuable, but I had limited time and energy, so I had to prioritize. When you're stressed and trying to just pass, you start cutting corners on things that feel less essential to your grade, even if they might help you learn more deeply.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. I needed to see a clear payoff for the effort I was putting in. With everything I'm balancing, I couldn't afford to spend hours on something that felt tangential. If I could see how it would help me complete an assignment or understand a key concept, I was more likely to engage with it fully.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required activities had urgency attached to them, so I'd push through even when I was exhausted. Optional ones got pushed to the bottom of my list and usually never got done. I felt guilty about that sometimes, but survival mode doesn't leave much room for extras.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download everything I needed and work from printed materials or my tablet without internet. I have notebooks full of handwritten summaries and practice problems that the LMS would never know about. I also used external resources like YouTube tutorials and free textbooks I found online to supplement what was provided.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes, reworking problems, or reading chapters from an external textbook. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I might have only logged in once that week to download something, but I was actually studying quite a bit. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual effort.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once, download it immediately, and then never open it on the LMS again. I preferred working offline because it was less distracting and I could study anywhere—on my commute, during lunch breaks at work. My engagement looked low online, but those materials were getting used extensively offline.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging became really hard. When my stress was at its peak—and it was high this semester—I'd feel paralyzed and unable to even open the LMS. Everything felt overwhelming. I'd look at my to-do list and just shut down. The sadness would creep in and I'd question why I was even doing this at my age.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. Workload from my job, confusion about some course concepts, and just pure fatigue from trying to do everything made the LMS feel like another burden. Some days I'd log in, stare at the screen, and log out without doing anything meaningful because I just couldn't process any more information.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open readings or start a video but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about bills, or work stress, or just feeling too sad to focus. I'd technically be 'accessing' materials but absorbing nothing. It's frustrating because the time spent didn't translate into actual learning during those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was anxiety—the bigger the task felt, the more I avoided it. Part of it was just being depleted from everything else in my life. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own problems. The CBT sessions helped me see this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing to get things done. It wasn't ideal, and my assignment completion rate shows that—I didn't always make it. But without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything at all given how overwhelmed I felt.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, reminded me I was capable. But critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral, especially when I was already feeling low. I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was afraid of what I'd see. When I finally looked, I'd either feel relieved or devastated.
Q18: The course structure with twelve online courses was honestly overwhelming for someone in my situation. Having so many modules to track made it hard to stay organized. I appreciated the flexibility of online learning since I work, but the sheer volume of content made it easy to fall behind and hard to catch up once I did.
Q19: The flexibility of accessing materials anytime was helpful for my schedule. But the lack of built-in structure or check-ins made it easy to drift away when things got hard. More scaffolding or smaller milestones might have helped me stay on track. Also, some of the navigation was confusing, which added unnecessary friction when I was already stressed.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying but struggling to produce quality work, or when they're anxious about submitting something that isn't perfect. You might spend hours reading and watching videos, genuinely trying to understand, but then freeze when it comes to actually writing and submitting. Fear of failure can be paralyzing.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still managed to pass because I was doing substantial work offline. I'd print materials, study from notes, use external resources—none of which showed up in the system. Low login numbers don't mean low effort; they might just mean someone studies differently.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show patterns of access and timing—when someone logs in, what they click on, submission timestamps. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and real comprehension. They can't capture someone reading printed notes at 2 AM or having a breakthrough understanding while walking to work.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in genuine effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. For me, it means wrestling with concepts until they make sense, even when it's hard. It's showing up mentally, not just clicking through screens. Real engagement is internal—it's about investment and persistence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to keep going this semester despite feeling sad and overwhelmed most of the time. It wouldn't show the hours of offline studying, the anxiety before every assignment, or the resilience it took to pass as a 29-year-old with competing responsibilities. It wouldn't show that I'm proud of myself for not giving up, even when everything felt impossible.
